title: Rally SDK 2.1 Charts & Reporting — Vector Application Gateway
topic: Rally SDK 2.1 Rally.ui.chart.Chart + StandardReport — config options, App Builder model, and implications for Vector custom app API gateway
summary: Rally SDK 2.1 chart and reporting components, App Builder model, and a design blueprint for Vector's equivalent Samantha SDK custom-app gateway.
content:
1. Executive Summary

Rally SDK 2.1 (CA Agile Central App SDK, built on Sencha Ext JS) provides a mature, config-driven framework for embedding interactive charts and reports inside a project-management product. Two components sit at the centre: Rally.ui.chart.Chart, which wires a data pipeline (Lookback API snapshots → calculator → Highcharts) into a single declarative config block; and Rally.ui.report.StandardReport, which wraps Rally's server-side report engine in an iframe with a clean, typed API surface. The App Builder adds scaffolding, a local dev server, and a single-file build/deploy pipeline. Together they demonstrate how a PM platform can expose a first-class developer extension layer. This paper extracts the patterns that are directly applicable to Vector's proposed Samantha SDK custom-app API gateway.

2. What These Components Are — Non-Technical Overview

Think of Rally's custom app system as a widget store for your project-management dashboard. An administrator creates a custom page (a blank canvas inside Rally), then drops in one or more apps written in JavaScript. Each app can display charts, tables, or reports using the same live data that Rally itself uses — portfolio items, stories, defects, velocity, burn rates — without any separate export or data-warehouse step.

Rally.ui.chart.Chart is the free-form chart widget. A developer specifies what data to pull (using Rally's time-series snapshot database), how to aggregate it (via a pluggable calculator class), and how to render it (via Highcharts config). The result can be a burndown, burnup, cumulative flow diagram (CFD), defect trend, or any other time-series visualisation.

Rally.ui.report.StandardReport is the high-level reporting shortcut. Instead of wiring up a data pipeline, a developer picks a named report type (e.g. ReleaseBurnup, VelocityChart, IterationCumulativeFlowDiagram) and provides a project/release/iteration context. Rally renders the report server-side and streams it into an iframe. No calculator or Highcharts knowledge is required.

The distinction maps cleanly to a two-tier API philosophy: a config API (StandardReport) that hides complexity behind sensible defaults, and a bare-metal API (Chart) that exposes full customisation. Vector needs both tiers.

3. Rally.ui.chart.Chart — Technical Reference

3.1 Class Hierarchy

  • Extends: Ext.Container
  • Alias: widget.rallychart
  • Mixin: Rally.app.Scopeable — injects getContext(), getProjectRef(), getWorkspaceRef(), timebox-scope helpers

3.2 Config Options

Config NameTypeDefaultDescription
storeTypeStringRally.data.lookback.SnapshotStoreClass name of the data store to instantiate. Override to use WSAPI store or custom store.
storeConfigObject | Object[]undefinedConfig passed to the store constructor. For SnapshotStore: find query, fields, hydrate, sort, pagesize. Can be an array to load multiple stores in parallel.
calculatorTypeStringundefinedClass name of the calculator. Must implement prepareChartData(snapshots) returning { series: [], categories: [] }. Typically extends Rally Lumenize calculators.
calculatorConfigObject{}Config passed to the calculator constructor. Includes start/end dates, granularity, field mappings.
chartConfigObject{}Raw Highcharts configuration object. Merged with SDK defaults. Supports all Highcharts chart types, axis config, tooltip formatters, plot options.
chartDataObjectundefinedPre-computed data object with series and categories keys. When provided, bypasses store and calculator entirely — useful for static or externally-fetched data.
chartColorsString[]['#E57E3A', '#E5D038', '#B2E3B6', '#3A874F']Ordered array of hex color values assigned to series in sequence.
loadMaskBooleantrueShow a loading spinner while data is being fetched.
updateBeforeRenderFunctionundefinedCallback invoked with (chartData) immediately before Highcharts renders. Use to mutate series data or inject annotations.
updateAfterRenderFunctionundefinedCallback invoked with (chart) after Highcharts renders. Use to add custom overlays or call Highcharts renderer directly.
queryErrorMessageString"No data was found based on the current chart settings."Message displayed when the store returns no results.
aggregationErrorMessageString"No data was returned by the calculator aggregation provided."Message displayed when the calculator returns empty series.
authorizationErrorMessageStringSDK defaultMessage displayed when the user lacks permission to read the queried scope.
haltedWorkspaceErrorMessageStringSDK defaultMessage displayed when the workspace is suspended.
serviceUnavailableErrorMessageStringSDK defaultMessage displayed when the Lookback API is unreachable.

3.3 Events

EventFired When
storesConfiguredStore instances have been created but not yet loaded.
storesLoadedAll stores have returned data.
storesValidatedStore data has passed internal validation (non-empty, authorised).
snapshotsAggregatedCalculator has produced series/categories output.
readyToRenderchartData is assembled; Highcharts has not yet painted.
chartRenderedHighcharts has finished painting the chart.

3.4 Public Methods

MethodReturnsDescription
refresh(newConfig)voidTears down existing stores and re-runs the full pipeline with an optionally updated config. Safe to call after user filter changes.
getChartWrapper()Highcharts WrapperReturns the Ext-wrapped Highcharts instance.
getChart()Highcharts.ChartReturns the raw Highcharts chart object for direct API access.

3.5 Data Flow Architecture

The chart component operates a four-stage pipeline:

  1. Store creation — storeConfig is passed to storeType constructor. For Lookback: a MongoDB-style find query is sent to /analytics/v2.0/service/rally/workspace/{oid}/artifact/snapshot/query.js. The store handles pagination, field hydration, and authentication (session cookie or zsessionid header injected by the SDK environment).
  2. Calculator aggregation — the raw snapshot array is passed to the calculatorType instance. Rally provides built-in Lumenize calculators (TimeSeriesCalculator, TimeInStateCalculator, TransitionsCalculator) or developers can extend Rally.data.lookback.calculator.BaseCalculator. Output must be { series: Highcharts.SeriesOptions[], categories: string[] }.
  3. Pre-render hook — updateBeforeRender(chartData) fires, allowing mutation of the assembled data before Highcharts sees it.
  4. Highcharts render — chartConfig is merged with the assembled data and passed to Highcharts. updateAfterRender(chart) fires with the live Highcharts instance.

3.6 Minimal Working Example

Ext.define('MyBurndownApp', {
  extend: 'Rally.app.App',
  launch: function() {
    this.add({
      xtype: 'rallychart',
      storeType: 'Rally.data.lookback.SnapshotStore',
      storeConfig: {
        find: {
          _TypeHierarchy: 'HierarchicalRequirement',
          _ProjectHierarchy: this.getContext().getProject().ObjectID,
          Iteration: { $exists: true }
        },
        fields: ['ScheduleState', 'PlanEstimate', '_ValidFrom', '_ValidTo'],
        hydrate: ['ScheduleState']
      },
      calculatorType: 'Rally.data.lookback.calculator.BurnCalculator',
      calculatorConfig: {
        startDate: this.getContext().getTimeboxScope().getRecord().get('StartDate'),
        endDate: this.getContext().getTimeboxScope().getRecord().get('EndDate'),
        granularity: 'day',
        field: 'PlanEstimate'
      },
      chartConfig: {
        chart: { type: 'line', zoomType: 'xy' },
        title: { text: 'Iteration Burndown' },
        xAxis: { title: { text: 'Date' } },
        yAxis: { title: { text: 'Story Points' }, min: 0 }
      }
    });
  }
});

4. Rally.ui.report.StandardReport — Technical Reference

4.1 Class Hierarchy

  • Extends: Ext.Component
  • Alias: widget.rallystandardreport
  • Alternate name: Rally.ui.StandardReport
  • Implementation: renders an <iframe> pointing to a Rally server-side report URL, constructed from the provided config.

4.2 Config Options

Config NameTypeDefaultDescription
widthNumberRequiredContainer width in pixels. Passed through to the iframe and the report engine.
heightNumberRequiredContainer height in pixels.
clickableBooleanfalseWhen true, clicking the rendered report navigates the parent window to the full-page report view inside Rally.
projectStringCurrent context projectRally project ref string (e.g. /project/12345678). If omitted, uses the app's current scope.
projectScopeUpBooleanContext defaultInclude parent projects in scope.
projectScopeDownBooleanContext defaultInclude child projects in scope.
reportConfigObjectRequiredNested config object for the report engine (see below).
reportConfig.reportStringRequiredReport type identifier. See supported report types below.
reportConfig.titleStringundefinedOverride the default report title displayed inside the chart.
reportConfig.subtitleStringundefinedOverride the default report subtitle.
reportConfig.widthNumberInherits outer widthWidth passed to the report engine (pixels).
reportConfig.heightNumberInherits outer heightHeight passed to the report engine (pixels).
reportConfig.legendString"show""show" or "hide" — controls legend visibility inside the rendered chart.
reportConfig.subchartString"show""show" or "hide" — controls the range-selector subchart below some report types.
reportConfig.xaxis_labelString"show""show" or "hide".
reportConfig.yaxis_labelString"show""show" or "hide".

4.3 Supported Report Types (reportConfig.report)

Report IdentifierDescription
BuildHealthBuild pass/fail health over time
BuildHealthByIterationBuild health broken down by iteration
CycleLeadTimeCycle time and lead time distribution
DefectsByPriorityOpen defects grouped by priority
DefectsByStateOpen defects grouped by state
DefectTrendDefect creation vs. resolution trend line
IterationBurndownRemaining work burndown for a single iteration
IterationBurnupAccepted work burnup for a single iteration
IterationCumulativeFlowDiagramCFD for a single iteration
IterationDefectsByPriorityDefects within an iteration by priority
IterationDefectsByStateDefects within an iteration by state
ReleaseBurnupAccepted work burnup across a release
ReleaseCumulativeFlowDiagramCFD across a release
ReleaseDefectTrendDefect trend within a release
StoryBurndownStory-points burndown (generic, non-timebox-scoped)
StoryBurnupStory-points burnup
StoryCumulativeFlowDiagramCFD for story points
TaggedStoryBurndownBurndown filtered by tag
ThroughputStories completed per iteration (velocity-style)
TopFilesByChangesSource files with most change activity
TopFilesByDefectsSource files with most associated defects
VelocityChartTeam velocity across rolling iterations

4.4 Events

EventFired When
loadThe report iframe has finished loading.
clickUser clicks the report (only when clickable: true).

4.5 Public Methods

MethodSignatureDescription
refreshrefresh(config, suppressMask)Updates reportConfig in-place and reloads the iframe.

4.6 StandardReport vs Bare-Metal Chart — When to Use Each

DimensionStandardReportRally.ui.chart.Chart
Setup complexityLow — pick report type + contextHigh — configure store + calculator + Highcharts
CustomisationLimited to show/hide flagsFull Highcharts API + pre/post hooks
Data accessServer-side only; no raw data exposedFull snapshot array available in calculator
Custom metricsNot supportedFully supported via custom calculator
ThemingRally brand onlyFull colour and style control
Offline/testRequires live Rally instanceSupports chartData override for static/test data
DependenciesRally report engine + iframeLookback API + Lumenize + Highcharts

5. The Rally App Builder Model

5.1 Scaffolding

The rally-app-builder Node.js CLI (npm package) generates a standard project skeleton:

rally-app-builder init --name=MyApp --sdk=2.1

Generated structure:
  App.js          # Main application class (Ext.define extending Rally.app.App)
  app.css         # App-specific styles
  config.json     # Build metadata: name, sdk version, javascript/css file lists
  app.html        # Dev preview host page (loads SDK + app scripts)
  deploy/
    App.html      # Built single-file artifact for deployment to Rally custom page

5.2 App Lifecycle

A Rally custom app is a class that extends Rally.app.App. The SDK owns the page lifecycle:

  1. Page load — Rally's platform injects the SDK bundle (sdk.js) into the custom page iframe.
  2. SDK boot — SDK establishes Rally.environment (workspace, project, user, permissions, timebox scope from parent page URL).
  3. App instantiation — Ext.define registers the class; SDK finds the app class and calls new MyApp({ context: environment.getContext() }).
  4. launch() — The primary entry point. Equivalent to React's first render or Next.js's getServerSideProps. Called once the DOM container is ready. Developers call this.add() to add child components.
  5. Context propagation — this.getContext() returns the live Rally.app.Context object containing project ref, workspace ref, user, and timebox scope. Child components that mix in Scopeable inherit this automatically.
  6. Timebox scope changes — If the parent page has a timebox filter (iteration or release picker), the app receives a timeboxscopechange event and typically calls this.refresh().
  7. Settings — Rally.app.AppSettings provides a declarative settings panel. Config is persisted server-side per-user per-app.

5.3 Authentication Inside the App

Authentication is entirely transparent to app code. Because apps run inside an iframe within the Rally platform, the user's existing Rally session cookie (ZSESSIONID) is automatically included in all same-origin WSAPI and Lookback API requests. The SDK's proxy layer (Rally.environment.getIoProvider()) routes all store requests through this session. App developers never handle tokens, headers, or login flows.

For external deployments (apps running outside the Rally iframe, e.g. on a company intranet portal), Rally provides an API Key mechanism: a long-lived token sent as the zsessionid HTTP header. API Keys do not expire with sessions and support SSO-enabled accounts. The LoginKey feature of App SDK 2.1 handles this case via a dedicated component.

5.4 WSAPI Query Model

Two data layers are available:

WSAPI (current state) — REST API at /slm/webservice/v2.0/. Returns current artifact state. Supports CRUD. Filtered via URL query params using query=(ScheduleState = "Accepted") syntax. Scoped by workspace/project context. Response includes TotalResultCount, PageSize, StartIndex, and a Results array of fully hydrated objects.

Lookback API (historical time-series) — Analytics endpoint at /analytics/v2.0/service/rally/workspace/{oid}/artifact/snapshot/query.js. Returns immutable snapshots representing every state change for every artifact. Query language is MongoDB-style JSON: find, fields, hydrate, sort, start, pagesize. Temporal operators include $gt, $lt, $gte, $lte, $in, $exists, $and, $or, and the special __At: "current" operator for point-in-time queries.

5.5 Build and Deployment

The build process concatenates App.js (plus any split JS files listed in config.json) and inlines CSS into a single App.html file. This file is copy-pasted into a Rally Custom Page HTML widget. The deployed app runs sandboxed in an iframe; it can only access data via the SDK's proxy (which enforces the session context) or explicit CORS-enabled external endpoints.

6. The Rally Lookback API — Query Reference

6.1 Endpoint

GET/POST https://rally1.rallydev.com/analytics/v2.0/service/rally/
         workspace/{workspaceOID}/artifact/snapshot/query.js

6.2 Key Query Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
findJSON ObjectMongoDB-style query filter. Required. Supports equality, comparison operators ($gt, $lt, $gte, $lte, $ne), array ops ($in, $exists), $and, $or, pattern ($regex), and __At for temporal queries.
fieldsArray | Object | BooleanFields to return. true = all fields (reduces max page size). false = default fields only (_id, ObjectID, _ValidFrom, _ValidTo, Project). Array or projection object for explicit selection.
hydrateString[]Fields to convert from ObjectID to readable name/object. E.g. ["ScheduleState", "State"].
sortJSON ObjectSort expression: {"_ValidFrom": -1}. Only effective on indexed fields.
startNumberZero-indexed offset for pagination.
pagesizeNumberRecords per page. Max varies by query complexity and field set.
compressBooleanMerge consecutive snapshots where requested fields are unchanged, extending time boundaries. Reduces result count for field-change tracking.
hydrateString[]Fields to resolve from OID to full object (name, dates for Iteration/Release; name for Project).
includeTotalResultCountBooleanDefault true. Set to false to skip count computation and improve response time.
removeUnauthorizedSnapshotsBooleanWhen true, silently filters out snapshots the user cannot read instead of returning an error.

6.2a Snapshot Database Retention Depth

What is documented / confirmed

No official Broadcom or CA Agile Central document publishes a numeric retention window for the Lookback API snapshot database. After exhaustive search across the official Rally analytics docs (rally1.rallydev.com/analytics/doc/), Broadcom TechDocs, and the Broadcom Knowledge Base, there is no stated policy of the form "snapshots are kept for N years." What is documented is the storage model: every artifact state-change creates a new immutable snapshot row; old snapshots are never deleted, only marked with a _ValidTo timestamp when superseded. Active (current) snapshots carry _ValidTo: "9999-01-01T00:00:00Z", which is an explicit sentinel for indefinite retention. The Rally analytics docs acknowledge a 15–60-second ingest lag for new snapshots but say nothing about pruning old ones. Documentation examples in Broadcom's own KB articles show _ValidFrom timestamps as far back as 2011, consistent with the analytics service having launched around that time and never culling data.

Architectural basis — Datomic

The indefinite-retention model has a concrete architectural explanation: Rally's backend uses Datomic as its primary database. Datomic is an append-only, immutable-fact database; data is never overwritten, only extended. A Rally engineering case study on the Datomic website confirms this directly: "If a document is created 5 years ago, we want to be able to track all the changes over that period of time. With Datomic, I get revision history. I don't worry about overwriting, just appending." This architectural choice — not a business policy — is why Rally's snapshot history is structurally indefinite: the database engine itself cannot overwrite or delete records without a separate purge operation, which Rally has not implemented. The Lookback API is effectively a read surface over Datomic's built-in temporal fact log. This is a strong architectural proof point that Vector's item_snapshots table design (Section 7.5) should follow the same append-only pattern — accepting inserts only, with valid_to updates written as new rows rather than mutations to existing rows.

Deleted artifacts — snapshots are permanent

An additional confirmed behaviour: when a Rally artifact (user story, defect, feature) is deleted, the Lookback API does not delete its snapshot rows. Instead, the current snapshot's _ValidTo is updated to the deletion timestamp, and the full snapshot chain remains queryable. If the artifact is restored from Rally's recycle bin, a new snapshot is created with a _ValidFrom equal to the restore timestamp — the gap is preserved as a visible discontinuity. This behaviour is documented in Broadcom KB article 143097 ("How to use Lookback API to find artifacts that were part of a deleted Timebox"). Implication for Vector: item_snapshots rows for soft-deleted items must be retained; a hard-delete of a portfolio item must not cascade to snapshot rows. This is consistent with the existing soft-archive invariant in Vector's schema.

What is undocumented but community-reported

No community forum thread (Broadcom Community, Stack Overflow, GitHub) has surfaced a confirmed retention cutoff. The consensus from practitioners who have queried mature Rally workspaces is that snapshots appear to go back to the workspace's first use of the analytics service — which for many enterprise accounts is 2011–2013 when Rally first rolled out the Lookback API broadly. There are no community reports of data silently disappearing after a period. The practical constraint is query performance, not retention: the Rally documentation warns that sorting on unindexed fields across very large result sets "can place significant strain on the system and may be throttled or blocked," and best practice is to paginate with incremental _ValidFrom checkpoints rather than spanning unbounded date ranges. The Lookback API supports page sizes up to 20,000 objects — ten times the WSAPI limit — specifically to accommodate large historical queries.

Implications for Vector

Two distinct scenarios apply to a new Vector tenant that migrates from Rally:

  1. Day-one history depth. A team with a years-old Rally workspace will have all their snapshot history available immediately — burndown and CFD charts can render truthfully back to whenever that team started using Rally. Vector's Samantha SDK chart gateway (see Section 7.5) should accept an optional historyStart parameter so migrating teams can anchor their charts at the earliest available snapshot rather than at the Vector tenant-creation date.
  2. Trend-chart maturity for net-new tenants. A team that starts on Vector with no imported history will have zero snapshot depth on day one. Rally's own Velocity chart covers the last 10 completed iterations; industry consensus (from Rally docs and Agile practitioners) is that 3 iterations is a pattern, 6 is a planning baseline, and 10 is reliable. Vector's trend charts should display a "building history" placeholder state for the first 3 iterations and a "limited data" warning for iterations 4–5, removing those states only when 6+ completed iterations are present. This avoids displaying misleading flat or near-flat charts to new teams.

Post-Broadcom acquisition changes

No evidence was found of Broadcom changing the snapshot retention model after acquiring CA Technologies (2018) or after Rally's App SDK EOL announcement (October 2026). The EOL affects custom apps and the SDK, not the underlying analytics data platform or the Lookback API endpoint itself, which remains live.

Sources

  1. Rally Lookback API official docs — https://rally1.rallydev.com/analytics/doc/ — no retention window stated; indefinite _ValidTo sentinel confirmed
  2. Datomic: Rally's Story — https://www.datomic.com/rallys-story.html — confirms Datomic as Rally's database; append-only revision history quote
  3. Broadcom KB: Rally Lookback API — how to analyze results — https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/129468/rally-lookback-api-how-to-analyze-the-r.html
  4. Broadcom KB: Rally LBAPI — artifacts from deleted Timeboxes — https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/143097/rally-lbapi-how-to-use-lookback-api-to.html — deleted artifact snapshot permanence confirmed
  5. Broadcom KB: Rally WSAPI data extract recommendations — https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/127874/rally-wsapi-data-extract-recommendation.html — incremental pagination best practice cited
  6. Broadcom TechDocs: Velocity Chart — velocity-chart.html — 10-iteration display window confirmed
  7. RallyTools/Rally-Lookback-Toolkit (GitHub) — https://github.com/RallyTools/Rally-Lookback-Toolkit
  8. RallyCommunity/RallyAnalytics (GitHub) — https://github.com/RallyCommunity/RallyAnalytics — 20,000 object page-size limit confirmed

6.3 Response Envelope

{
  "_rallyAPIMajor": 2,
  "_rallyAPIMinor": 0,
  "Errors": [],
  "Warnings": [],
  "TotalResultCount": 4200,
  "HasMore": true,
  "StartIndex": 0,
  "PageSize": 200,
  "ETLDate": "2026-04-27T22:00:00.000Z",
  "Results": [
    {
      "_id": "...",
      "ObjectID": 12345678,
      "_ValidFrom": "2026-04-01T09:00:00.000Z",
      "_ValidTo": "2026-04-02T09:00:00.000Z",
      "_PreviousValues": { "ScheduleState": "In-Progress" },
      "ScheduleState": "Accepted"
    }
  ]
}

The ETLDate field is the synchronisation watermark. When paginating multi-page results, include _ValidFrom: { $lte: ETLDate } in subsequent requests to prevent drift during live ingestion.

7. Vector Application Gateway — Design Blueprint (Samantha SDK)

7.1 Strategic Analogy

Vector's product is to portfolio management what Rally is to agile delivery. The same two-tier SDK pattern applies:

  • Tier 1 — Config API (samantha.chart.StandardChart): equivalent to Rally's StandardReport. Developers pick a named chart type (e.g. portfolio-burn, layer-allocation-cfd, model-velocity), provide a portfolio model context, and get a rendered chart. Zero data wiring.
  • Tier 2 — Bare-Metal API (samantha.chart.Chart): equivalent to Rally's Rally.ui.chart.Chart. Developers specify a data query, an optional aggregation function, and a Recharts/Vega/Chart.js config. Full control.

7.2 Proposed React Component API

StandardChart (Tier 1)

import { StandardChart } from 'samantha/chart';

<StandardChart
  chartType="portfolio-burn"
  modelId={portfolioModel.id}
  dateRange={{ start: '2026-01-01', end: '2026-06-30' }}
  width={800}
  height={400}
  legend="show"
  onClick={(point) => router.push(`/item/${point.itemId}`)}
/>

Chart (Tier 2 — bare metal)

import { Chart } from 'samantha/chart';

<Chart
  queryConfig={{
    table: 'item_snapshots',
    filter: { model_id: portfolioModel.id, zone: 'strategy' },
    fields: ['schedule_state', 'weight', 'valid_from', 'valid_to'],
    hydrate: ['schedule_state']
  }}
  calculatorFn={(snapshots) => myAggregator(snapshots)}
  chartConfig={{
    type: 'area',
    xAxis: { type: 'time' },
    series: [] // filled by calculatorFn
  }}
  colors={['#E57E3A', '#E5D038', '#B2E3B6']}
  onBeforeRender={(data) => data}
  onAfterRender={(chartInstance) => {}}
/>

7.3 App Lifecycle for Vector Custom Apps

Vector custom apps are React components registered in the samantha.apps registry. The host page (a Vector custom page of type custom-app) mounts them inside a sandboxed <div> (not an iframe, since Vector owns the full-stack rendering). The lifecycle mirrors Rally's but uses React idioms:

  1. Registration — App developer calls samantha.apps.register({ id: 'my-app', component: MyApp, manifest: {...} }) in their app bundle.
  2. Context injection — Vector passes a context prop: { tenantId, portfolioModelId, userId, permissions, dateRange, theme }. Equivalent to Rally's Rally.app.Context.
  3. Data access — App calls samantha.data.query(config), which routes through Vector's backend API gateway (authenticated with the user's session, scoped to the tenant and portfolio model in context). No token handling in app code.
  4. Settings persistence — samantha.settings.get/set(key, value) persists per-user per-app settings to Vector's user_preferences table. Matches Rally's PreferenceManager.
  5. Theme compliance — Context provides the active theme slug. App uses samantha.theme.getCSSVar(name) to resolve design tokens. Ensures white-label compatibility.

7.4 Authentication and Authorisation Model

Rally's in-app authentication (session cookie transparent to developers) and its API Key mechanism (for external apps) provide a good two-mode template:

ModeRally equivalentVector implementation
In-platform appsSession cookie (ZSESSIONID), auto-injectedNext.js session cookie (vector-session), injected by the API gateway middleware — app code never sees it
External / CI integrationsAPI Key (zsessionid header)Samantha API Key: long-lived token, scoped to tenant + portfolio model, sent as Authorization: Bearer <key> header
Scope enforcementPer-workspace, per-projectPer-tenant, per-portfolio-model, per-permission-role (padmin / gadmin / viewer)
Token granularityUser-level only (no resource-level scoping)Opportunity to add: read-only vs read-write, specific model IDs, expiry date — Rally's lack of granularity is a documented gap

7.5 The Vector Snapshot Store — Equivalent to Rally's Lookback API

Rally's Lookback API powers all time-series charts by storing immutable snapshots of every state change. Vector needs an equivalent to support burndown, CFD, and velocity charts over portfolio model items. The table name in Vector's schema would be item_snapshots (or model_snapshots), with columns mirroring Rally's critical fields:

Rally Lookback fieldVector equivalentNotes
ObjectIDitem_id (UUID)FK to items table
_ValidFromvalid_from (timestamptz)Snapshot start time
_ValidTovalid_to (timestamptz)Snapshot end time; NULL = current
_PreviousValuesprevious_values (JSONB)Fields that changed in this snapshot
ScheduleStateschedule_stateE.g. Backlog / In Progress / Done
PlanEstimateweight or plan_estimateNumeric, used for burn calculations
_ItemHierarchyitem_ancestry (UUID[])Materialised path for hierarchy queries
_ProjectHierarchymodel_id (UUID)Portfolio model scoping

7.6 Named Chart Types for StandardChart (Tier 1)

Derived from Rally's StandardReport type list, adapted to Vector's domain:

Chart Type IDDescriptionRally analogue
portfolio-burnupAccepted items / weight burnup across a portfolio model's target dateReleaseBurnup
portfolio-burndownRemaining work burndown for a portfolio modelStoryBurndown
portfolio-cfdCumulative flow by schedule state for all items in a modelReleaseCumulativeFlowDiagram
layer-allocationWeight distribution across strategy / execution / reserve zones over timeNo direct equivalent — Vector-native
velocityItems completed per review period (rolling)VelocityChart
defect-trendRisk items created vs. resolved over timeDefectTrend
cycle-timeDistribution of time from creation to completionCycleLeadTime

7.7 API Gateway Design — Safeguarding

Rally's WSAPI rate limits (12 concurrent requests per user; account suspension for abuse; no hourly/daily cap) are a minimal safeguard model. Vector should implement a more structured gateway, especially if third-party developers are given API Keys:

ControlRally approachVector recommendation
Concurrency limit12 simultaneous requests per userConfigurable per API Key; default 5 concurrent for external keys, unlimited for in-platform session
Rate limit (time-based)None documented1,000 req/min per API Key for read; 100 req/min for write — enforce at gateway middleware (e.g. Redis token bucket)
Scope enforcementWorkspace + project, inherited from sessionAPI Key carries explicit allowed_model_ids[] and permission_level (read / read-write); enforced in every query handler before DB call
Token lifecycleNo expiry; manual revocation onlyConfigurable expiry (default 90 days); automatic rotation prompt; instant revocation via gadmin panel
Audit trailNot documentedLog every API Key request to api_audit_log: key_id, endpoint, status, duration, tenant_id
Abuse responseAccount suspension (no prior warning)Rate-limit header (X-RateLimit-Remaining), 429 response with retry-after, warning email before suspension

7.8 Developer-Facing Documentation Granularity

Rally SDK 2.1's docs (JSDuck-generated, ExtJS-native) demonstrate the right information density: each class has an inheritance chain, every config option has name/type/default/description, every method has a signature and return type, every event has a trigger description. The source code is also browseable alongside the API docs. Vector's Samantha SDK documentation should match this pattern:

  • Component reference: React prop tables with name / type / default / description (equivalent to Rally's config tables).
  • Data query reference: full field list for each queryable table, hydration options, filter operators.
  • Lifecycle guide: step-by-step equivalent of Rally's App Builder guide — from samantha init to deployed custom page.
  • Live examples: in-product equivalent of Rally's runnable example panels — each StandardChart type should have a live preview in the Developer section of Vector.
  • Error reference: named error codes with resolution guidance, analogous to Rally's error message config options on the Chart component.

8. EOL Context and Timeliness

Rally's App SDK and custom pages are being deprecated with end-of-life set for October 31, 2026. The stated reason is that the SDK relies on "outdated technology that is no longer actively supported, posing unacceptable security and performance risks." Sixteen new native widgets are being built to replace the most common app types. This is a cautionary data point for Vector: a custom app layer built on a technology framework (ExtJS) with no upgrade path created a migration cliff. Vector should design the Samantha SDK on web-standard primitives (React, fetch, JSON) with no proprietary framework dependency, ensuring long-term maintainability.

9. Gaps — Items Not Verified at 95% Confidence

  • Rally.ui.chart.Chart — private method internals: the full list of internal store-loading and validation methods could not be extracted from the JS-rendered source page. The public API (configs, events, public methods) is confirmed at 95%+.
  • SnapshotStore — precise page-size limits: Rally documentation states limits "vary by query complexity and fields parameter" without publishing a table. The general behaviour (lower limits when fields: true, higher with projection) is confirmed.
  • Lookback API throttling: documentation states "throttling planned for future implementation" but no current hard limits are published. The 12-concurrent-request WSAPI limit does not apply to the Lookback API endpoint.
  • App Builder guide — exact HTML skeleton: the guide is JS-rendered and content was not retrievable from the HTML source. The scaffold structure described in Section 5.1 is synthesised from npm package documentation and README references, not direct page extraction.
  • Rally.data.lookback.calculator.BurnCalculator: referenced in community examples but not confirmed in SDK 2.1 source as a built-in; it may be a community-contributed class. The base calculator pattern (BaseCalculator.prepareChartData) is confirmed.
  • StandardReport — iteration and release ref params: the iframe URL construction logic references project, release, and iteration but the exact parameter names for passing iteration/release refs to reportConfig are not confirmed beyond the documented project top-level config.

Analytics Manual — Extended Reference

The following material was sourced from the official Rally Lookback API manual at rally1.rallydev.com/analytics/doc, Broadcom Knowledge Base articles, and the RallyCommunity/RallyAnalytics and RallyTools/Rally-Lookback-Toolkit open-source repositories. It covers topics not already documented in Section 6 above.

Complete Operator Reference — Additions to Section 6.2

Section 6.2 documents the core comparison and logical operators. The full supported set also includes:

Operator / SyntaxDescriptionNotes
{a: {$type: 2}}Match documents where field a is of BSON type 2 (string). See bsonspec.org for full type table.Useful for distinguishing numeric OIDs from string values in mixed-type fields.
{"a.b": 10}Dot-notation query on embedded documents — matches where a is a sub-document with field b equal to 10.Enables querying into _PreviousValues sub-fields, e.g. {"_PreviousValues.ScheduleState": "In-Progress"}.
$ninNot supported. Not available in the Rally Lookback API query engine.Use multiple $ne clauses combined with $and as a workaround.
$norNot supported.Rewrite using De Morgan's law with $and and $ne.
$notNot supported.Use $ne or $exists: false for negation.
$allNot supported.Use multiple equality clauses instead.

FormattedID — Special Case Field

FormattedID (e.g. "US1234", "DE567") does not exist in the internal snapshot schema. It is a virtual field composed at query time by combining _UnformattedID (the numeric ID) and _TypeHierarchy (the artifact type). The API transparently translates a FormattedID query into its component parts:

// Query as submitted:
{ FormattedID: "DE1234" }

// Internally translated to:
{ _UnformattedID: 1234, _TypeHierarchy: "Defect" }

Operator restriction for FormattedID: Because FormattedID is virtual, only a subset of operators is supported when querying by it:

  • Supported: $and, $or, $ne, $in, $exists
  • Not supported: $lt, $lte, $gt, $gte, $all, $nin, $nor

If you need range or exclusion queries, rewrite using _UnformattedID (numeric) or ObjectID directly.

Drop-Down Fields — Query and Hydration Behaviour

Rally drop-down fields (e.g. ScheduleState, State for Portfolio Items) are stored internally as integer ObjectIDs, not strings. Two important behaviours:

  1. Query-time string conversion: You may query a drop-down field using its string value (e.g. { ScheduleState: "Accepted" }). The API silently converts the string to the corresponding ObjectID before executing the query. This means the string must exactly match a valid Rally value.
  2. Portfolio Item State and comparison operators: When using $lt / $gt on a Portfolio Item State field, comparison is performed on the State ObjectID, not the display name or ordinal index. ObjectIDs are not guaranteed to follow the same ordering as the state workflow sequence. To use range comparisons correctly: query the State object via WSAPI to retrieve ObjectIDs for each named state, then construct your LBAPI query using those numeric OIDs.

By default, hydration is off — drop-down fields return as raw ObjectIDs unless the hydrate parameter is specified. When hydrated, the API resolves the ObjectID to the human-readable string value.

Hydrating _PreviousValues Sub-Fields

Hydration of _PreviousValues requires individual sub-field specification. You cannot hydrate the entire _PreviousValues object at once. Each field within _PreviousValues that you want resolved must be listed separately using dot notation:

// Correct — hydrate individual _PreviousValues fields:
"hydrate": ["ScheduleState", "_PreviousValues.ScheduleState"]

// Incorrect — NOT supported:
"hydrate": ["_PreviousValues"]

The same field name must appear in both the main hydrate list and the _PreviousValues. form if you want both current and previous values resolved to strings.

Hydrating Iteration, Release, and Project

When hydrated, timebox and project references return as objects with useful attributes:

  • Iteration: Returns object with Name, StartDate, EndDate, ObjectID.
  • Release: Returns object with Name, ReleaseStartDate, ReleaseDate, ObjectID.
  • Project: Returns object with Name, ObjectID, _ref. Specify "hydrate": ["Project"].

Without hydration, all three fields return as raw integer ObjectIDs.

Date Format Details

All dates in the Lookback API are UTC and use ISO-8601 format. The API is lenient about trailing zero components — you may omit lower-order units that are zero:

// Full form:
"2011-02-01T00:00:00.000Z"

// Equivalent shortened forms:
"2011-02-01T00:00:00Z"
"2011-02-01T00Z"
"2011-02-01"

Timezone handling: All times are stored in UTC. If you want midnight in a local timezone, you must shift the time yourself before submitting. For example, midnight Eastern Time (UTC-5) on 2011-01-01 should be submitted as "2011-01-01T05Z".

Full ISO-8601 support on temporal fields (_ValidFrom, _ValidTo, __At) includes week numbers, ordinal dates, and day-of-year notation.

The __At Operator — How It Works Internally

The __At temporal shorthand is translated internally into a two-clause _ValidFrom/_ValidTo range query. For a given timestamp T:

// Submitted:
{ "__At": "2026-01-15T00Z" }

// Internally translated to:
{ _ValidFrom: { $lte: "2026-01-15T00Z" }, _ValidTo: { $gt: "2026-01-15T00Z" } }

This guarantees exactly one snapshot is returned per ObjectID for the given moment in time (since the model guarantees non-overlapping validity windows). The special value "current" translates to _ValidTo: "9999-01-01T00:00:00Z".

Null Values in Snapshots

Null (empty / "No Entry") field values follow a specific storage rule:

  • Fields with null values are not stored in the snapshot document — they are simply absent.
  • Exception: When a field changes from a non-null value to null, the _PreviousValues of the new snapshot will explicitly record the old non-null value. The new snapshot itself will not include the field (since null = absent).
  • When a field changes from null to a non-null value, _PreviousValues will explicitly state the previous value as null: { "_PreviousValues": { "MyField": null } }.

Indices and Sort Performance

The Lookback API sorts results correctly only when the sort key is an indexed field. Sorting on an unindexed field may execute but the sort will be silently ignored or produce unreliable ordering. Known indexed fields include:

  • _ValidFrom (default sort direction: ascending)
  • _ValidTo
  • ObjectID
  • Project
  • _TypeHierarchy
  • _ProjectHierarchy
  • _ItemHierarchy

The default sort (when none is specified) is { "_ValidFrom": 1 } — ascending by snapshot creation time. For large result sets, always sort on an indexed field and paginate using incremental _ValidFrom checkpoints rather than high start offsets, to avoid deep-skip performance degradation.

Authentication — Three Supported Modes

The Lookback API supports three authentication approaches, in order of preference:

  1. API Key via header (recommended for scripts and external apps): Send the API Key as the zsessionid HTTP header. Rally API Keys begin with an underscore character and must be included with the key: zsessionid: _abc123mykey. Generate keys in Rally's My Profile → API Keys panel. These keys do not expire with the user session and work with SSO-enabled accounts.
  2. Session cookie (browser / in-platform apps): When running inside the Rally platform iframe, the ZSESSIONID session cookie is automatically present and applied to all same-origin requests. No explicit authentication code is required.
  3. Username / Password (legacy / Node.js scripts): Pass credentials via environment variables RALLY_USER and RALLY_PASSWORD (used by community toolkits) or via HTTP Basic auth. This approach does not work for SSO accounts and is not recommended for production integrations.

Integration identification headers (optional but recommended): Scripts and integrations should include these courtesy headers to aid Rally's support team in diagnosing issues:

X-RallyIntegrationName: My Integration
X-RallyIntegrationVendor: My Company
X-RallyIntegrationVersion: 1.0.0

GET vs POST

Both HTTP GET and POST are supported. For GET requests, query parameters are URL-encoded in the query string. For POST requests, the body is a JSON object with top-level keys matching the parameter names (find, fields, hydrate, sort, start, pagesize, etc.). POST is preferred for complex queries where URL length limits may be reached.

Example GET:

curl --header "zsessionid:_myKey" \
  "https://rally1.rallydev.com/analytics/v2.0/service/rally/workspace/12345/artifact/snapshot/query.js\
  ?find={%22ObjectID%22:777}&fields=[%22_ValidFrom%22,%22_ValidTo%22]&start=0&pagesize=200"

Pagination — HasMore and Page Walking

The response envelope includes a HasMore boolean field (in addition to the TotalResultCount documented in Section 6.3). The correct pagination loop pattern is:

start = 0
pagesize = 2000
while True:
    result = query(find=..., start=start, pagesize=pagesize)
    process(result["Results"])
    if not result["HasMore"]:
        break
    start += pagesize

Performance note: For large datasets, prefer filtering by an incremental _ValidFrom window (e.g. checkpoint the last _ValidFrom seen and use _ValidFrom: { $gt: checkpoint }) rather than incrementing start into millions of records. The former uses an index; the latter forces a full-collection skip.

_SnapshotNumber Field

Each snapshot in the Lookback API includes an internal _SnapshotNumber field — a monotonically increasing counter that identifies the sequence in which snapshots were created for a given ObjectID. Including "_SnapshotNumber" in the fields parameter returns this value and can be useful for distinguishing the first snapshot of an artifact (creation event: _SnapshotNumber: 0) from subsequent update snapshots.

_PreviousValues — Querying State Transitions

The canonical pattern for detecting a state transition (e.g. when a user story entered In-Progress) is to query for snapshots where the current value is the target state AND the previous value was something else:

{
  find: {
    _TypeHierarchy: "HierarchicalRequirement",
    ScheduleState: "In-Progress",
    "_PreviousValues.ScheduleState": { $exists: true },
    "_PreviousValues.ScheduleState": { $ne: "In-Progress" }
  },
  hydrate: ["ScheduleState", "_PreviousValues.ScheduleState"]
}

The _PreviousValues field is only present on a snapshot if at least one field changed in that snapshot event. The first snapshot for a newly created artifact has no _PreviousValues.
